content.summary_short: Turn discovery into a clean recommendation without overpromising, collapsing scope, hiding delivery ownership, or skipping client readiness.
content.summary_long: This episode teaches how to recommend a single lane or bundle, explain why, name assumptions, and keep scope honest so the next step stays commercially useful and operationally safe.
content.start_here: Listen first, then use the slides to practice single-lane versus bundle recommendations.
content.key_line: Discovery should end in direction, not in vagueness.

slides.title: Discovery must turn into direction
slides.goal: Show why recommendation is the next skill.
bullets: Good sales process ends in a clear next-step recommendation.
If discovery ends in fog, the prospect still feels lost.
Clear direction is part of commercial value.

slides.title: Single division versus bundle
bullets: Some opportunities have one obvious primary lane.
Others need a primary lane plus support lanes to actually solve the problem.
The recommendation should match the real shape of the need.

slides.title: Lumina-led is not Lumina-delivered
slides.goal: Protect the ownership line during recommendations.
bullets: Lumina can lead the commercial framing and still point delivery elsewhere.
That is a strength, not a weakness.
A clean recommendation makes the delivery owner explicit.

slides.title: Common bundle patterns
bullets: Lumina + Nexus for positioning plus systems.
Lumina + EchoVerse for rollout plus sound or release experience.
Lumina + Horizon for campaign packaging plus visual proof.

slides.title: Hold back when needed
bullets: Sometimes the strongest recommendation is narrower than the lead expects.
If the real need is simple, do not inflate it into a vague mega-bundle.
Honest scope improves trust and close quality.

slides.title: Explain the why
bullets: The recommendation language should tie directly to the problem and outcome.
If you cannot explain why the lane fits, the recommendation is not ready.
A clear why keeps internal handoff stronger too.
Name assumptions and open decisions before anyone treats the recommendation as a promise.

slides.title: Worked recommendation example
bullets: Lead: a regional service company asks for a new site, but discovery reveals weak offer clarity, poor lead follow-up, and manual intake.
Clean recommendation: Lumina + Nexus. Why: Lumina clarifies the commercial offer and message; Nexus handles the intake flow and automation layer.
What to avoid promising: a full rebuild of everything at once before the scope and operations constraints are understood.
slides.takeaway: The best recommendation names the lane, the reason, and the guardrail.

slides.title: Recommendation formula
slides.goal: Give the learner a repeatable structure.
bullets: Problem -> recommended lane or bundle -> reason -> next step.
This formula keeps the recommendation short, useful, and hard to fake.
Use it before you send anything externally.
Add guardrail language: based on current discovery, pending scope review.

slides.title: Recommendation guardrails
bullets: Say what is recommended now, not everything AeroVista could eventually do.
Separate a likely path from a committed deliverable.
Use plain limits: pending scope review, access review, timeline review, or partner confirmation.
slides.takeaway: A recommendation earns trust when it is clear about both fit and limits.

references.blurb: Use the original lane docs plus ByteCast standards to reinforce this episode.

advance.label: EP-LUM-106
advance.description: Complete EP-LUM-105 gates, then continue into Turn Qualified Interest into Growth.

quest.title: EP-LUM-105 practice checklist
quest.prompt: Use the checklist to turn discovery notes into a clean recommendation.

items.text: Say the recommendation formula once from memory.
Choose one sample lead and decide whether it is single-lane or bundle.
State one thing the team should not promise before scope review.
Explain why Lumina-led does not mean Lumina-delivered.

quiz.prompt: Pass the recommendation check before you move into growth motion and follow-up.

questions.q: What is the safest reading of Lumina-led?
choices: Lumina may lead commercially while another division delivers
Lumina should hide the delivery owner
questions.explain: Commercial leadership and delivery ownership are distinct.

choices: When the real need spans more than one lane
questions.explain: Bundles should follow need shape, not habit.

questions.q: What does strong scope discipline look like?
choices: Narrow the recommendation when that is the honest fit
Promise everything and sort it out later

questions.q: In the worked service-company example, why is Lumina + Nexus plausible?
choices: Because the lead only needs aerial footage
Because the need includes message clarity plus intake-flow structure
Because every lead should become a two-lane bundle
questions.explain: The recommendation follows the discovered combination of commercial and systems problems.

questions.q: Which phrase keeps a recommendation client-safe?
choices: This locks the final outcome before review
Based on current discovery, this is the likely path pending scope review
We can do everything at once
questions.explain: Client-safe recommendations name fit while preserving scope, timeline, and review boundaries.
